It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 34 of about 59 target actions so far.

OK, so clicking on the yellow square didn't change anything, and pressing UP didn't move the yellow square. The yellow square is still at [20:21, 24:25].
Let me think about this differently. The yellow square is in the top gray box, which is a 3x3 grid of 2x2 cells. The cells are:

So the yellow square is in the middle-center cell of the top gray box.
Let me think about the game again. The right shapes have a "stepped" pattern. The colored squares are in the gray boxes. The left blocks rearrange when I click on the right shapes.
Maybe the game is about "painting" the left blocks to match the right shapes. The colored squares are "brushes" that paint the blocks. But I haven't seen any evidence of the blocks being painted.
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the game is about matching the left blocks' colors to the right shapes' colors. The red shape is red, and the blue shape is blue. The left blocks include a red block and a blue block.
Maybe the goal is to have the red block adjacent to the red shape, and the blue block adjacent to the blue shape. But the left and right panels are separated by a white column divider, so the blocks can't be adjacent to the shapes.
Let me try yet another approach. Maybe the game is about arranging the left blocks to form a specific pattern, and the right shapes are just visual hints. The "stepped" pattern has:
```
  . X .
  . X .
  X X X
```
The left blocks need to form a similar pattern. The blocks are:
- Gray: 4x4
- Red: 4x6 (horizontal) or 6x4 (vertical)
- Dark red: 4x4
- Blue: 4x4
- Checkerboard: 4x4

- Bottom: blue rect (4x4) at left

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me restore the initial configuration by clicking on the blue shape
# This should swap checkerboard and blue rect back to their original positions
predict(expect_change=True, note='Click on blue shape to restore initial configuration')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 35, 'col': 48}])

ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("=== Left blocks after clicking blue ===")
for r in range(18, 44):
    line = ascii_lines[r][7:22]
    if any(c not in 'c' for c in line):
        print(f"  {r}: {line}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        r0, r1 = min(p[0] for p in b), max(p[0] for p in b)
        c0, c1 = min(p[1] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"\nLight green: [{r0}:{r1},{c0}:{c1}]")

print(f"\nLevel completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")

# Now let me also check the initial configuration for comparison
init_frame = history[0].frame
init_ascii = init_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\n=== Initial left blocks ===")
for r in range(18, 44):
    line = init_ascii[r][7:22]
    if any(c not in 'c' for c in line):
        print(f"  {r}: {line}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
